item.title: Splatoon Raiders Review – A More Narrowly-Targeted Spinoff that Hits the Looter-Shooter Spot
item.description:
Game Info
Splatoon Raiders
July 23, 2026
Platform
Nintendo Switch 2
Publisher
Nintendo
Developer
Nintendo

Nintendo has typically taken a grab-bag approach with the Splatoon franchise , mixing different modes of gameplay together into a single package -- single-player, PvP multiplayer, co-op base defense, and more -- but with the upcoming Splatoon Raiders , they're taking more of a focused approach. This spinoff is primarily a single-player adventure (although there is the option to play co-op) that zeroes in on combat and a looter-shooter-style progression system.

Is Splatoon Raiders a successful expansion of this fishy series? Or has Nintendo gone and painted themselves into a corner? Swim on to find out...

Splatoon Raiders casts players as the Mechanic, a (mildly) customizable avatar who happens to be piloting the helicopter for the idol group Deep Cut when the whole crew is drawn into a massive storm and crash land on the mysterious Spirhalite Islands. After setting up a home base, the members of Deep Cut turn their attention toward harvesting the various treasures that dot the islands. Getting home is also somewhere on the to-do list, but really, treasure is the top priority. You're recruited to go on treasure raids, which allow you to upgrade your gear and island hideout and perhaps even uncover the secrets of the Spirhalite Islands, which seem to have a connection to those nasty Salmonids that keep invading the Splatlands.

Don't expect a super complex or gripping plot from Splatoon Raiders, although the game comes to a suitably epic conclusion and fleshes out the personalities of Deep Cut as the story progresses and you increase your bond with specific members of the crew. As has always been the case with Splatoon games, there's also a fair amount of weird lore to be uncovered here, for those who are interested in such things.

The core activity in Splatoon Raiders is, as you might have guessed, raids, which come in a variety of different flavors. Your standard raid drops you onto a usually-linear map (although there is another basic raid variant where objectives are more spread out) alongside your chosen Deep Cut member who rides around in a handy-dandy Exploration Bot. Your goal is to track down and dig up a series of Spirhalite Shard caches, but your Deep Cut partner can only crack them open with their bot’s drill once you've cleared all the Salmonids in the immediate vicinity, and, of course, tons of them spawn as soon as you come upon a stash. Splat the Salmonids, dig up the shards, rinse and repeat a couple more times and the raid is done. Along the way you'll also find new weapons, which have levels and star rarities attached (higher rarity weapons come with bonus status buffs), and plenty of crafting materials.

The actual process of splatting baddies is about what you'd expect if you've played Splatoon’s Salmon Rush mode before. Splatter enemies with a variety of familiar weapon types, from the basic Splattershot to more creative options like Rollers and paint-dealing Katanas, and turn into squid form to swim through your own ink for greater maneuverability and to refill your ink supply. This time around, players can also equip gadgets (up to three of them eventually) that are on a cooldown timer and allow you to do things like summon a protective ring of ink-spreading satellites or unleash an inky boomerang on enemies. Additionally, collecting Power Eggs from fallen foes charges up your Deep Cut bot partner, eventually allowing you to unleash a powerful battlefield-clearing Showstopper attack.

Your Salmonid opponents, which include basic cannon fodder fishies and more dangerous Boss Salmonids, are largely lifted from pash Salmon Rush modes, although a few new ones have been added. Enemy AI is pretty much the same as it's always been, with the typical Salmonid strategy being to spawn in large numbers and charge mindlessly at you. Battles can be intense, but there's a limit to how strategic they get.

In addition to the standard raids, there are a few other mission types, which usually involve drilling underground and more closely resemble the base-defense gameplay of past Salmon Run modes. Salmonid Dens challenge you to earn a certain amount of Power Eggs within a time limit, while Buried Ruins are similar, but three levels deep, culminating in a battle against a Seasoned Salmonid (a salt-crusted extra-tough Boss Salmonid). Meanwhile, hidden Sampler Dungeons task you with making your way through a stage using a specific weapon and/or gadget and typically focus a bit more on platforming and simple puzzle solving.

Standard raids are generally fairly easy, with the level design between enemy encounters being very straightforward. Battles aren't that daunting either, as you're given plenty of space to back off and catch your breath if you're temporarily overwhelmed. The underground Salmonid Dens and Buried Ruins are significantly more challenging, sometimes frustratingly so, although even if you fail a mission you get to keep all your experience, weapons, and items, so no run is ever truly wasted. The Sampler Dungeons are actually some of the more interesting stages in the game as their level design feels a bit more creative, but they're usually fairly quickly bested. Oh, and without giving too much away, there are also some bigger, badder boss battles in this game, which are a definite highlight. All of this can be played with up to three other players online or via local wireless, and there's even a Dark-Souls-esque feature where you can call in another player to help you on a particularly tricky bit.

While Splatoon Raiders does a solid job of mixing things up in terms of mission variants, there's no denying the game feels a bit more one-note than past entries in the franchise. The large majority of your time is spent doing one thing – repeatedly splattering large hordes of Salmonids. Early on, the game rolls out new Salmonid varieties at a fairly steady pace, but at a certain point, Nintendo runs out of new enemy types and just starts throwing more of them at the player to increase the difficulty level. Eventually, the game does start to feel a bit repetitive.

This isn't helped by visuals that don't quite have the gonzo pop you expect from this franchise. Aside from the ink you spread on them, most stages have a somewhat muted look and could do with a bit more variety. The game's soundtrack is typical Splatoon fare, which is to say, it complements the action well enough, but probably won't leave you humming.

If Splatoon Raiders can sometimes feel repetitive, the overall experience remains engaging due to the snackable bite-sized length of missions and the game's rather' involved looter-shooter progression system. In addition to collecting a wide assortment of weapons, players can tinker with their gadgets by applying parts to them that you either find during Raids or craft using harvested materials and shards. Your ink tank can be upgraded in various ways as you level up (HP up, damage up, more gadget part slots etc.) and levels up separately based on the total number of Power Eggs you've collected. Your rank will also increase as you earn more money from treasure, conferring further bonuses. Point is, there's an almost constant stream of dopamine-generating goodies and upgrades coming your way, which keeps you pushing through even when you're up against a mission you may not be in love with.

And while Splatoon Raiders may be a spinoff that's selling for only $50 digitally, there's still quite a hearty amount of content on offer here. The diversity of said content may be a bit lacking compared to most major Nintendo releases, with only three main biomes and not a ton of new Salmonid types, but still, it took me a solid 12 hours to roll credits on the game. Add potential replay in the form of harder "Spicy" versions of raids and some endgame content I won't spoil and this is a game you could get over 20 hours out of without trying too hard. And who knows how long getting the elite loadout of your dreams might take? If you enjoy splatting Salmonids, this fish fry doesn't have to end anytime soon.

This review was based on a copy of Splatoon Raiders provided by publisher Nintendo.

You can find additional information about our standard review process and ethics policy here.

8
WCCFTECH RATING

Splatoon Raiders

Splatoon Raiders sets its sights on inking Salmonids to such a singular degree that the game can sometimes feel a bit one-note, but a solid array of different mission variants and a gratifying dopamine-dispensing progression system has that “just one more raid” effect. While Splatoon Raiders doesn't offer as much variety as earlier entries in the franchise, it does its one thing quite well, and those who jive with this new looter-shooter approach will likely find themselves caught in this game's net for some time.

    Pros
  • Splatting Salmonids can be intense
  • Solid variety of raid types
  • Compelling progression
  • Good replayability
  • Can play the game co-op
    Cons
  • Level design is straightforward
  • Presentation is somewhat drab
  • Could use more new Salmonids
Buy for from Amazon The links above are affiliate links. As an Amazon Associate, Wccftech.com may earn from qualifying purchases.

item.title: Beelink EQi Wildcat Lake 304 “Intel Core 3 304” Mini PC Review – 18A Efficiency King For The Masses
item.description:
Product Info
Beelink EQi 304
July, 2026
Type
Mini PC
Price
$659.99

Beelink was established in 2011 & is regarded as one of the top sellers in the Mini PC category at Amazon. The company has a diverse portfolio of Mini PCs, Players, and gadgets, but the core focus is on the Mini PC segment, which includes both Intel and AMD options.

While we have looked at several of Beelink's Mini PC options in the past, ranging from AMD & Intel CPUs, the company has introduced a new Intel model recently, the EQi 304 , which is based on Intel's Wildcat Lake SoCs. This is the first Mini PC to feature Wildcat Lake, and as such, we've been looking forward to really trying this out, as this platform has the potential to disrupt the entry-tier segment.

Beelink is offering the EQi 304 in three configurations, which feature the same Intel Core 3 304 chip and 512 GB storage but with different memory configurations: 16 GB, 24 GB, and up to 32 GB. We got the one with 24 GB memory, which has a retail price of $659 US.

Beelink EQi 304 "Wildcat Lake" Mini PC - Specifications At A Glance

In terms of specifications, the Beelink EQi 304 is equipped with the Intel Core 3 304. This is the most entry-level chip within the Wildcat Lake "Core Series 3" lineup, and it immediately tells us who this PC is designed for. For regular office, business, and productivity tasks, Wildcat Lake is said to be a great fit, with its highly efficient design that is a stripped-down version of Panther Lake on the same 18A architecture.

So, talking about Intel Core Series 3 or Wildcat Lake SoCs , they feature the same processor architectures as Panther Lake (Cougar Cove P-Cores / Darkmont E-Cores), the same GPU architecture as Panther Lake (Xe3), and the same NPU as Panther Lake (NPU5). The only thing that's changed, which is the case with every cost-optimized variant, is the difference in the overall specs and capabilities of each chip. That is why the chip has just two chip tiles versus several on Panther Lake SoCs.

The Intel Core 3 304 uses a mix of Intel 18A and TSMC N3B nodes. The primary compute tile with CPU cores, GPU cores, NPU, etc, is based on Intel's leading tech, while the I/O tile is based on the TSMC process tech. It features a total of 5 cores, of which one is based on the Panther Cove P-Core architecture, while the rest of the 4 cores are based on the Darkmont LPE-Core architecture. The CPU carries 6 MB of Intel Smart Cache.

The P-Core is clocked at 1.5 GHz base and 4.3 GHz boost, while the LPE cores are clocked at 1.4 GHz base and 3.3 GHz boost. The chip has a base power rated at 15W, a minimum assured power of 10W, and a maximum turbo power of 35W.

For the iGPU, Intel's Wildcat Lake CPUs pack the Xe3 architecture. The configuration specific to the Core Ultra 3 304 features just 1 Xe3 core, which clocks up to 2300 MHz, and supports a max res of 4K@60hz. While the Xe3 architecture is featured, the graphics capabilities are dialed back for Core Series 3, and you don't get either Ray Tracing Units or the full DX12 Ultimate support.

Intel does retain Multi-Frame Generation support up to 4x through its latest Arc drivers. The GPU is rated at 9 TOPs while the NPU offers a peak of 15 TOPs. The new NPU supports the latest AI frameworks such as OpenVINO, Windows ML, DirectML, ONNX RT, and WebNN.

Mini PC Internals

With the CPU covered, let's talk about the rest of the specifications that the Mini PC has to offer. Intel made Wildcat Lake with affordability in mind, and to achieve this, they had to look at the platform level.

For this, memory is the biggest deal, and Intel has resorted to a single-channel memory layout while still offering both LPDDR and DDR options. In today's memory-constrained world, this should be the single biggest thing that Intel has done to give everyday users the best cost-to-memory option in a laptop.

First, we have the memory, which comes in the form of 24 GB of DDR5-5600. The Wildcat Lake SoCs support up to LPDDR5x-7467 and DDR5-6400 memory. Our unit came with a single DDR5-5600 memory module from Micron with a 24 GB capacity. The specific part number for the RAM is "CT24G56C46S5.M8C1" and operates at CL46-45-45-90-135 timings (1.10V).

It doesn't end here; storage has also been configured for cost optimizations. The use of UFS 3.0 SD card standards makes for lower power and lower cost, while offering decent space sizes, while the reliance on Gen4 SSD makes for a balanced yet high-speed approach vs the more expensive Gen5 options.

Our unit came with 512 GB of UFS 3.1 Flash storage. This is YMTC "TMUSAB4TF3D1C1" flash, which has a maximum rate speed of 2000 MB/s (Read) and 1900 MB/s (Write). The PC has two PCIe Gen4 NVMe slots, with one rated at x2 speeds and the other one rated at x1, so you can definitely expand the storage capabilities.

IO on the Mini PC comes in the form of a USB 3.2 10 Gbps port, 3.5mm Audio Jack USB Type-C 10 Gbps port, a power button, and a power indicator on the front. The backside features two USB Type-C Thunderbolt 4 ports rated at 40 Gbps, two USB 2.0 ports, one HDMI port rated at 4K @ 60 Hz, two LAN ports (10GbE+2.5GbE), and an AC Power Jack.

Talking a little bit about the BIOS, there are plenty of options such as tuning the CPU, GPU, DRAM, and Fan settings. There are options to change the power limits from the reference 15W PL1, 35W PL2, up to 50W. You can also adjust the PL4 limits and set the specific amount of memory used by the iGPU/NPU, though those options are also available in the Intel Graphics Command Center.

Beelink EQi 304 "Wildcat Lake" Mini PC - Unboxing & A Closer Look

The Beelink EQi 304 Mini PC comes in a white colored cardboard package, which has a simplistic look.

The back of the box has some basic specifications that list the specific configuration for this model. We got the Intel Core 3 304 variant with 24 GB of memory & 512 GB of storage capacity. Opening the box, you get a nice "Thank you" message on the plastic wrap.

Within the box, you can find the Mini PC in the top compartment, along with a guide/warranty booklet, the power cable, and an HDMI cable underneath the box. There's no adapter since the Mini PC comes with a built-in power supply.

Beelink has made use of its updated design for the EQi 304. It comes in grey color that has the Beelink logo etched in the center and hexagonal textures on the front. The PC measures 126 x 126 x 44.2mm and weighs 500 grams.

The top and sides are fully enclosed except for the rear and bottom of the chassis, which have vents to push hot air out of the unit, which is dissipated by the cooling solution. There's a single fan that is used to cool the Mini PC, while a heatsink with a singular heatpipe attaches over the main chip.

The front I/O on the Beelink EQi 304 "Wildcat Lake" includes a USB-C 10 Gbps port, a USB 3.2 10 Gbps Type-A port, a 3.5 mm audio jack, a Power button, and an LED indicator.

The back I/O on the Beelink EQi 304 "Wildcat Lake" includes two Thunderbolt 4 Type-C 40 Gbps ports, two USB 2.0 ports, a single HDMI port, dual LAN ports, and an AC Power Jack. The vents are also placed on the back.

To access the internals, you have to unscrew the four screws hidden behind tiny rubber covers. There's a rubber pull tab which lets you easily lift the rear plate.

Internally, the first things that you notice are the single DDR5 DIMM, a PSU, and a heatsink that covers two additional PCIe Gen4 slots along with the MediaTek WiFi card. The topmost PCIe 4.0 slot is connected via a cable that connects to the mainboard, while the lower slot is located on the PCB. Both slots feature heatsinks.

The power supply is an 85W design, which means you don't need to carry an adapter to boot the PC up, just a wall-mount cable would do the trick, though it would have been even nicer if this PC offered Type-C power support.

You do have the option to upgrade the memory and storage besides the built-in UFS 3.1 512 GB Flash, but in terms of memory, you can go up to 32 GB, 48 GB, and 64 GB. The MediaTek module offers Wi-Fi 6E and Bluetooth 5.2 support.

Beelink EQi 304 "Wildcat Lake" Mini PC - CPU Performance

Now, coming to the CPU performance, there isn't a whole lot to expect from a chip with just 5 cores and 5 threads, more so given the fact that just one of those cores is a P-Core while the rest are LP-E cores, more power-tuned versions of regular E cores.

Starting with the synthetics, the Intel Core 3 304 scored 332.2 points in CPU-Z's single-core and 2767.1 points in the multi-core bench. There were times when the single-core scores went up to 500+ points, but after an initial stress run, the score mostly settled at around 300-400 points.

The Cinebench 2024 scores were seen at 105 points in single-core and 351 points in multi-core, which is nothing much to write about.

Moving over to Geekbench 6, we saw a single-core score of 2378 points and a multi-core score of 6241 points. 3DMark's CPU Profile tests show a single-core score of 954 points and a max thread score of 3147 points.

PCMark 10 was unable to complete the full benchmark, citing an "OpenCL" driver error, but the "Essentials" suite was decent in terms of performance. The Core i3-1220P "Alder Lake" CPU with 10 cores scores around the same, and that has 12 threads with a base TDP of 28W.

While these scores may look a bit underwhelming, we have to understand that Wildcat Lake is designed as a replacement for the older N-series lineup, and an N305 with 8 cores and 8 threads has nearly half the single-core performance, and lacks much behind in multi-core tests, at the same wattage, which shows the leap that the new LPE and P cores are making in the tests we conducted.

Beelink EQi 304 "Wildcat Lake" Mini PC - AI Synthetic Performance

We tested the AI performance through Geekbench AI. The CPU bench under the ONNX framework was alright, but the NPU side under the "OpenVINO" framework saw a big boost. This is a very decent and lightweight NPU that can supplement the majority of AI-related local use cases.

Beelink EQi 304 "Wildcat Lake" Mini PC - GPU Synthetic Performance

Now we are going to look at the GPU performance, and before we present to you the gaming numbers, we first have to show you the synthetic performance. Now, once again, the Wildcat Lake does feature the same Xe3 architecture, but they are stripped-down versions, and feature only up to 2 Xe3 cores. The Core 3 304 is the only chip that features just one Xe3 core; as such, don't expect lots of potential here, but we really want to see what a single Xe3 core manages to offer.

For synthetic tests, we used 3DMark, and the only four benchmark results we were able to get were Steel Nomad, Wild Life, and Fire Strike. We will explain in a bit why that's so, but first, let us look at the results:

Looking at some of these benchmarks, especially the last Steel Nomad score, we can instantly see the scaling. A single Xe3 core scores 141 points, while 12 Xe3 cores at a similar TDP on the Arc G3 Extreme gave us around 1400 points. And the X9 388H ends up around 1700 points. So yeah, you can tell almost linear scaling for each core that's added to the mix.

Now for the rest of the benchmarks, Speed Way, Port Royal, and Solar Bay failed to run due to missing RT hardware and DXR1.1/DX12U/VulkanRT support, while TimeSpy didn't run because Wildcat Lake didn't support SSSE 3.

Understandable though since having just one GPU core makes the addition of RT pointless. Now, we will take a look at gaming performance.

Beelink EQi 304 "Wildcat Lake" Mini PC - 720p Gaming Performance

With the synthetic performance out of the way, we can start taking a look at pure gaming numbers, and first, we tested Cyberpunk 2077 at 720p at Low, with XeSS Upscaling set to "UltraPerformance" and Frame-Gen enabled. We only got around 50-70 FPS, & without frame-generation, the performance dropped to 28-33 FPS.

We tried many other games, even Skyrim Special Edition, which ran at 720p Low natively at around 60 FPS in interiors and 40-50 FPS outdoors.

Red Dead Redemption 2 at 720p Low with Performance Upscaling (FSR) ran at around 30-35 FPS at night time. We weren't able to get daytime footage, but we managed low 30s in that setting.

No Man's Sky ran decently at 720p "Standard" with XeSS "Performance" upscaling. The FPS was over 60 in spaceflight, while on the planet, the FPS dipped in the 40-50 FPS range.

Forza Horizon 6 at 720P with Low Settings and XeSS "Ultra Performance" gave us around 30 FPS. With Frame-Gen, this could have been pushed higher, but even running the game at 30 FPS on just 1 Xe3 GPU core is impressive. Besides that, we also tried Baldur's Gate III, LEGO Batman, Marvel Rivals, CS2, and Minecraft.

You can use Wildcat Lake SoCs for playing classic and older titles at around 30 FPS at 1080p with low settings, and around 60 FPS at 720p with low settings. The chip also does a nice job at emulation. Video playback was also great, so besides gaming, the single Xe3 GPU core handles most of your routine and everyday tasks quite well.

Beelink EQi 304 "Wildcat Lake" Mini PC - Power & Thermals

As for the power figures, the Beelink EQi 304 is rated at 15W PL1 and 35W PL2 out of the box. The PL4 is rated at 80W, but the chip hardly ever crosses 25W. In CPU tests, the chip stayed around 20 Watts but did initially pull over 30 Watts for a few milliseconds before returning to the original 20 Watts state. The idle power draw was very low at sub-2W, and video playback was around 2.5-3.5 Watts. In gaming, we saw that the GPU only consumed 5-6W of power while the CPU consumed around 18-20W.

As for thermals and noise, the idle temperatures were around 34-35C while the chip peaked at 87C when the initial 30W limit kicked in before settling down to 60-70C. There was also a 10C difference between the P-Core and the E-Cores. The P-Core peaked at 87C while the E-Cores peaked at 74C.

Conclusion - Mini Daily Driver With Mass Appeal

With this, we have finally tested our first Wildcat Lake system, sure it's not a laptop, which this chip is primarily designed for, but even in a Mini PC, it is an efficient offering that gets most of your daily work done.

Beelink definitely made use of those cost-saving analogies that are a must in today's world, where component prices are ever rising. Although the retail price is still $659, you are getting 24 GB, and that alone retails for $360 ( Newegg listing ). That leaves $299 US for the rest of the PC, which offers a 512 GB UFS 3.1 storage solution, which is around $70-$90 these days if you buy it separately off retail shelves. That leaves us with $210-$230 for the PC.

Now for the performance, Wildcat Lake was meant to be an Alder Lake-N replacement, and the Core 3 304 with its single P-Core and 4 LPE cores offers all the capabilities a regular PC user will ever want. It's not a multi-tasking powerhouse, and it's not a machine designed with gamers in mind. For everyday productivity and office tasks, the chip is enough. In terms of gaming, you can expect to play a few older titles at 720P with 60 FPS, but don't expect a lot since most games will not work due to lack of RT/DX12U/VLKRT support.

But an iGPU running at 5W with just 1 Xe core delivering around 60 FPS in Skyrim Special Edition was a surprise, albeit it was at 720p and low settings.

Some positives about the Beelink EQi 304 include its dual Thunderbolt 4 ports, dual LAN ports, including 10 GbE LAN, dual M.2 SSD slots (Gen4), and a built-in PSU. One thing I would like to say is that the UFS 3.1 storage is decent, but not all that great. Windows encountered major hiccups when running on it, and I had to switch over to an M.2 drive, which ran perfectly.

Design-wise, it's a super-light PC with whisper-quiet operation. The CPU never surpassed 25W in power, and most workloads were running between 15-20W. We are thoroughly impressed with the Wildcat Lake SoC in its overall capabilities, and Beelink has done a great job with its EQi 304 series.

The following are the pros and cons of the Beelink EQi 304 Mini PC:

  • +Wildcat Lake runs efficiently (sub-25W operation)
    +Decent Temperatures & Whisper-quiet cooling
    +1 Xe3 GPU @ 5W can play Skyrim at 60 FPS (at 720p)
    +Dual PCIe 4.0 NVMe slots
    +Dual Thunderbolt 4 Ports (Type-C)
    +Dual LAN ports (10GbE+2.5GbE)
    +Nice Compact chassis
  • -UFS 3.1 storage can be a bit slow in Windows 11
    -CPU & GPU are only meant for entry-level productivity tasks
    -Single-Channel memory support
    -High Price (Due to DRAM/NAND shortages)

If the prices weren't as bad as they are right now due to NAND/DRAM shortages, we would expect these PCs to be retailing for $300-$350, making them a great choice for mainstream masses looking for a lightweight and efficient system.

You can find additional information about our hardware review process and ethics policy here.

item.title: AMD Ryzen 7 7700X3D 4.5 GHz “3D V-Cache” CPU Review: The Budget X3D Champ For AM5
item.description:
Product Info
AMD Ryzen 7 7700X3D
16th July, 2026
Type
CPU
Price
$329.99

AMD's AM5 platform has been a solid entry, featuring some of the best chips on the market and a wide variety of entry-level and high-end options to select from.

Despite the AM5 launch, AMD was still focusing on releasing the more mainstream and budget-tier solutions for the older AM4 socket, which still holds the majority share, but the company has recently started to launch affordable CPU options in its AM5 segment. The Ryzen 5 7600X was one such example.

But with recent component price hikes, it has become even more vital to support older lineups. The commitment to AM4 remains strong as ever with the re-release of the Ryzen 7 5800X3D 10th Anniversary Edition, and as AMD pushes its AM5 platforms' longevity by 2029+, the company has a brand-new entry.

Meet the AMD Ryzen 7 7700X3D. This is the brand-new 3D V-Cache chip on the block, which promises some strong gaming potential with Zen 4's solid efficiency figures. The Ryzen 7 7700X3D has the same core and cache configuration as the 7800X3D, one of the best X3D options in the PC market right now, while the 9800X3D and 9850X3D push the capabilities to the next level.

The Ryzen 7 features just one CCD and a singular 3D V-Cache stack, but being a single CCD design is the best approach towards gaming workloads, as the thread scheduler doesn't have to go between the dual CCDs to find the best fit for gaming applications. AMD has made huge strides in optimizing its Ryzen scheduler for Windows OS, and we have seen these optimizations come to full light with the recent game releases.

In terms of specifications, the AMD Ryzen 7 7700X3D 3D V-Cache CPU features the same 8-core/16-thread count as the 7700X, but it has 64 MB more L3 cache, totaling 96 MB. The chip features a base clock of 4.0 GHz and a boost clock of 4.5 GHz. This makes it the slowest of the three Zen 4 8-core chips out there:

  • Ryzen 7 7700X - 4.5 / 5.4 GHz
  • Ryzen 7 7800X3D - 4.2 / 5.0 GHz
  • Ryzen 7 7700X3D - 4.0 / 4.5 GHz

The AMD Ryzen 7 7700X3D has an official price tag of $329. This makes it the cheapest 8-core 3D V-Cache chip on the AM5 platform so far, and that value is going to entice a larger AM4 audience to make the jump.

Contents

  • 1. Intro
  • 2. Test Setup
  • 3. CPU Performance Tests
  • 4. Discrete GPU Gaming Benchmarks
  • 5. CPU Power Consumption Tests
  • 6. CPU Temperatures
  • 7. Conclusion - More X3D Options For AM5 Builders
Next page

You can find additional information about our hardware review process and ethics policy here.

item.title: Team Group T-Force GA PRO Gen5 2 TB NVMe SSD Review – Tuned Specs For Entry-Level Gen5 Builders
item.description:
Product Info
TeamGroup T-Force GA PRO 2 TB Gen5
2025
Type
SSD
Price
$329.99

Back in 2023, T-Force by TeamGroup introduced its first Gen5 SSD solution, the CARDEA Z540. Based on the Phison E26 controller, this SSD offered up to 12.4 GB/s speeds, making it a solid option back then. At the moment, there are plenty of Gen5 SSD controllers in the market, Phison has its new E28 and E31T, the SMI SM2508 is doing great, and InnoGrit, along with Samsung, has its own solutions offering really fast storage capabilities on NVMe products.

TeamGroup has also streamlined its product family over the years, now leveraging the G-series branding in "GE", "GC", and "GA". The "GE" series offers up to 14 GB/s speeds, the "GA" series offers up to 12.5 GB/s speeds, and the "GA" series offers up to 10 GB/s speeds. There's also the "G" series SSDs, but these are Gen4-based and offer up to 5 GB/s speeds. As these controllers and NAND Flash improve, the speeds for Gen5 SSDs will continue to be uplifted further, and T-Force has been upgrading its products with solid releases all year round.

For today's review, we will be taking a look at T-Force's GA PRO SSD. Our sample is a 2 TB model which retails for $179.99 US.

T-Force GA PRO 2 TB PCIe Gen5 NVMe SSD

The T-Force GA PRO 2 TB Gen5 SSD is part of the high-end T-Force series, and it sits right below the "GC" series. The T-Force GA PRO Gen5 SSDs are based on the NVMe SSD form factor and feature the YMTC 232-Layer 3D TLC NAND flash, which offers capacities of up to 4 TB. The T-Force GAseries SSDs are based on the Innogrit IG5666 controller, which is in use by some high-end Gen5 drives at the moment.

FEATURES

  • Gen5 Is beyond Limits, Offering Next-gen Speed Performance
  • Multi-core Solution for Performance Evolution
  • Data Security Isolation Mechanism
  • New Generation Error Correction Technology
  • Patented graphene heatsink for enhanced heat dissipation and system operating stability
  • S.M.A.R.T. Monitoring System
  • Be Eco-friendly and Conserve the Earth
  • Patented Graphene Heat Sink
  • Invention patent number in the US: US11051392B2
  • Innovation patent number in Taiwan: I703921
  • Utility model patent number in China: CN 211019739 U
  • Patented S.M.A.R.T. Software
  • Innovation patent number in Taiwan: I751753

As for the specs, the T-Force GC PRO NVMe SSDs will come with the standard NVMe 2.0 PCIe Gen 5 x4 interface in the M.2 2280 form factor. It will feature 1 TB, 2 TB, and up to 4 TB capacities with a rated speed of up to 10.0 GB/s (Sequential Read) and 8.5 GB/s (Sequential Write).

T-Force also ships the drivers with a Graphene pad, which we have seen to offer up to 3-4C temperature reduction versus standard drivers that have stickers on top of the PCB. As for heatsink variants, T-Force doesn't offer them, but they do offer cooling solutions which we recently tested here , offering good value and performance.

T-Force GC PRO Gen5 SSD Lineup Specifications:

T-Force GA PRO 2 TB Gen5 NVMe SSD – Unboxing and Closer Look

The T-Force GA PRO SSD comes in a fairly standard and small package with a black and red color scheme. The labels do a great job of telling what SSD you are getting since the capacities, speeds, and warranty are clearly mentioned along with a picture of the device itself.

The T-Force "GA" series SSD box includes a warranty booklet, the Graphene heatpad, and the SSD itself. All drives are held within a plastic shell container.

Out of the package, all T-Force GA PRO series SSDs come in the standard M.2 2280 form factor and feature a black PCB, which is great since some mainstream drives come with blue-colored PCBs.

The SSD doesn't feature any sticker, but the Graphene heatpad can easily be attached to it.

The T-Force GA PRO 2 TB features the InnoGrit IG5666 Gen5 SSD controller along with four YMTC 232-layer 3D TLC NAND Flash modules and two SK Hynix DDR4-3200 DRAM modules with 2 GB capacity. The bottom side of the PCB has a regular sticker.

Test Setup

Our test platform for this review consisted of an Intel Core i9-13900K processor, which ran at stock clocks on the MSI Z790 ACE motherboard. The platform was coupled with 32 GB of DDR5 memory from G.Skill (Trident Z5/ 16 GB x 2) and the MSI MEG Ai1300P PSU. For graphics, I used the MSI GeForce RTX 4090 SUPRIM X. This rounds up as a high-end platform for tests with modern-day SSDs. The MSI Z790 ACE specifically houses a single Gen 5.0 x4 NVMe slot that sources its lanes from the Raptor Lake CPU.

As the industry’s leading provider of high-performance storage & network connectivity products, ATTO has created a widely accepted Disk Benchmark freeware to help measure storage system performance. As one of the top tools utilized in the industry, Disk Benchmark identifies performance in hard drives, solid-state drives, RAID arrays, as well as the host connection to attached storage. Top drive manufacturers, like Hitachi, build and test every drive using the ATTO Disk Benchmark.

The ATTO Disk Benchmark performance measurement tool is compatible with Microsoft Windows. Use ATTO Disk Benchmark to test any manufacturer's RAID controllers, storage controllers, host bus adapters (HBAs), hard drives, and SSD drives, and notice that ATTO products will consistently provide the highest level of performance to your storage.

Conclusion - Similar To GC With A Lower Price

Team Group GA series SSDs match the higher-end GC series, as both are based on the same specs. Both feature Innogrit's IG5666 Gen5 controller and YMTC NAND Flash, and there is no major difference besides some clock speed tuning here and there. We were impressed with the GC series, so the same conclusion applies here. Users can expect a very responsive storage SSD, backed by a 5-year warranty, and solid performance.

Due to recent NAND shortages, SSD prices have gone up significantly. The T-Force GA is currently listed at $329, which is much higher than what the GC PRO 2 TB used to retail for ($179) a year ago when we tested it. Furthermore, despite several years going by on the Gen5 platform, we have yet to see any major performance uplifts when going from Gen4 to Gen5; even Gen3 SSDs are mighty capable for the vast majority of applications these days.

With that said, if you can find the GA or GC PRO 2 TB SSDs under $200 US, they will be a great deal; if not, just stick with your current storage solution and wait for prices to drop or some nice deals to show up.

You can find additional information about our hardware review process and ethics policy here.

item.title: Biwin Opal DW100 RGB DDR5 48 GB (2 x 24 GB) 6000 MT/s CL28 EXPO Memory Kit Review – Solid EXPO Kit But Way Overpriced Right Now
item.description:
Product Info
Biwin Opal DW100 DDR5 RGB (48 GB 6000 CL28)
June, 2026
Type
Memory Kit
Price
$699

In 2026, we live in the worst kind of memory crisis that the consumer market has ever faced. Some people call it the RAMmageddon, and others call it the RAMpocalypse. Whichever way you want to call it, one thing is set: shortages created by massive demand in the AI and data center segment have led to a severe hike in prices across all memory segments.

This came at a time when memory speeds were seeing rapid updates. We saw speeds surge past 9000 MT/s, and some kits were hitting close to 10,000 MT/s. We saw the launch of CUDIMMs and CQDIMMs, plus capacities that pushed 2-DIMM platforms to 256 GB. But these are unobtanium these days. Even entry-level kits are out of reach.

Many manufacturers these days are doing the opposite of what they were doing last year. They are sticking with 6000-6400 MT/s speeds but with super tight latency and timings. We have seen vendors double down on this approach with AMD's new EXPO ULL standard, and others stick by the default EXPO specs to offer enhanced capabilities with the latest DRAM ICs. So today, we will be testing Biwin's premium Opal DW100 DDR5 RGB kit .

Biwin Opal DW100 RGB DDR5-6000 48 GB Memory Kit Specifications

The Biwin Opal DW100 DDR5 RGB memory kit features a premium design and build quality. The name Biwin might sound new to some of you, but they have been around for a long time, & have their own manufacturing and testing facilities. The company also operates its more Enthusiast-aimed brand, called Origin Code.

For this particular testing, Biwin sent us its Black Opan DW100 DDR5 RGB "BXL54860DW1-D28FB" 48 GB (24 GB x 2) kit, which is rated at 6000 MT/s. The memory kit consists of dual 24 GB DIMMs, which make up a total of 48 GB in capacity. Following are the full specifications of the kit I'll be testing.

  • Series: Opal DW100 RGB
  • Module Type: 288 Pin Unbuffered DIMM On-Die ECC
  • Capacity: 24GBx2
  • Frequency: 6000 Mbps
  • Data Transfer Bandwidth: 48.000 MB/s (PC5 48000)
  • Dimensions: 136.19 x 50.24 x 8.40 mm
  • Latency: CL28-36-36-72-108
  • Tested Voltage: 1.40V
  • Heat Spreader: Three-Fin Aluminum heatspreader

The memory kit has a tested latency spec of CL28, and the timings are 28-36-36-72-108 for this specific kit. The voltage set at reference is 1.40V, and the memory kit comes in an unbuffered package with a non-ECC (On-Die Error Correction) design.

The memory kit is fully compliant with AMD EXPO and Intel's XMP standards. DDR5 memory compatibility differs from motherboard to motherboard, so make sure that your board is high-end and rated to support high-clock-speed memory sticks.

As for detailed specs, there is just one EXPO profile that the Opal DW100 kit comes with. The first offers the aforementioned timings. Another thing to note is that the modules are based on the SK Hynix M-Die.

Biwin Opal DW100 RGB DDR5-6000 Memory Kit Unboxing and Close-Up

The Biwin Opal DW100 DDR5 RGB series memory comes in a standard cardboard package. The box has pictures of the memory in black and white colors, and lists various RGB software that it is compatible with. A stick also points out the specifications of the kit you are getting.

The back of the package lists that the kit comes with an express warranty and also offers a glance at the various features.

Within the package are two DDR5 DIMMs.

Outside the package, the Biwin Opal DW100 DDR5 RGB memory kit is a bulky and high-end design. The front has a three-fin heatsink design.

The kit we got comes in black. The memory also comes in all-black and all-white flavors.

Biwin Opal DW100 memory modules feature a full-coverage diffuser that covers the sides, too. It offers 16.8 million RGB colors, 10+ Lighting controls, and 8 distinct lighting zones.

The heat spreader is made using a 2mm aluminum alloy structure. Within this is a thermal pad that connects to each IC and the PMIC. Biwin also employs a Temperature Sensor, while the outside features an electrophoresis coating.

The frame of the memory DIMMs is of a high-class design that uses a matte finish.

Biwin also states that it is using an unlocked PMIC, which dynamically adjusts voltages on the DW100 series to maintain a stable power supply during overclocking. Furthermore, each DIMM features a 10-layer PCB that minimizes electromagnetic interference.

The following is how the memory looks when installed on an X670E motherboard.

Wccftech Memory Test Setup

For testing, I used the latest AMD Ryzen 7 9850X3D on the Gigabyte X670E AORUS Extreme motherboard using the latest BIOS with the AGESA 1.3.0.1b Path A firmware, which supports EXPO ULL memory kits. We also used an RTX 5090 for our tests.

DDR5 Memory Kit Performance Tests

I have separated the performance tests into two sections. The first section consists of synthetic and general workload tests, while the second section focuses on gaming performance and how different games are affected by higher-clocked memory sticks.

Gaming Performance Tests

Gaming applications generally don't see huge gains with different memory kits, but DDR5 has enabled a huge gain in memory frequency, which can impact gaming performance. Moving from the stock 2133 Mbps (DDR4) to 6000+ Mbps can have a huge effect on performance, and the improved CAS timings on some of the high-end SKUs can result in better overall FPS and system responsiveness. This is especially true for AMD's Ryzen CPUs and 3D V-Cache offerings that do benefit a lot from tighter-timed DDR5 kits.

Conclusion - Hand-Picked Hynix Dies For Tuners

Biwin's hand-selected Hynix M-dies show potential; their out-of-the-box performance is really good, and given that the company offers a wide range of options in its Opal DW100 stack, they are a good option for those who want to upgrade to DDR5 platforms.

The kit we tested does come at a high price of $699, and while the prices are very high for DRAM at the moment, the competition offers 48 GB DDR5-6000 (CL40) kits at over $700, making Biwin's option sound much better. The premium build quality, with a fantastic RGB diffuser, also looks great. We just hope that DDR5 prices return to normal as soon as possible so more PC users have access to these exceptional memory products.

You can find additional information about our hardware review process and ethics policy here.

item.title: Biwin Opal DW100 RGB DDR5 48 GB (2 x 24 GB) 6000 MT/s CL28 EXPO ULL Memory Kit Review – Solid EXPO Kit But Way Overpriced Right Now
item.description:
Product Info
Biwin Opal DW100 DDR5 RGB (48 GB 6000 CL28)
June, 2026
Type
Memory Kit
Price
$699

In 2026, we live in the worst kind of memory crisis that the consumer market has ever faced. Some people call it the RAMmageddon, and others call it the RAMpocalypse. Whichever way you want to call it, one thing is set: shortages created by massive demand in the AI and data center segment have led to a severe hike in prices across all memory segments.

This came at a time when memory speeds were seeing rapid updates. We saw speeds surge past 9000 MT/s, and some kits were hitting close to 10,000 MT/s. We saw the launch of CUDIMMs and CQDIMMs, plus capacities that pushed 2-DIMM platforms to 256 GB. But these are unobtanium these days. Even entry-level kits are out of reach.

Many manufacturers these days are doing the opposite of what they were doing last year. They are sticking with 6000-6400 MT/s speeds but with super tight latency and timings. We have seen vendors double down on this approach with AMD's new EXPO ULL standard, and others stick by the default EXPO specs to offer enhanced capabilities with the latest DRAM ICs. So today, we will be testing Biwin's premium Opal DW100 DDR5 RGB kit .

Biwin Opal DW100 RGB DDR5-6000 48 GB Memory Kit Specifications

The Biwin Opal DW100 DDR5 RGB memory kit features a premium design and build quality. The name Biwin might sound new to some of you, but they have been around for a long time, & have their own manufacturing and testing facilities. The company also operates its more Enthusiast-aimed brand, called Origin Code.

For this particular testing, Biwin sent us its Black Opan DW100 DDR5 RGB "BXL54860DW1-D28FB" 48 GB (24 GB x 2) kit, which is rated at 6000 MT/s. The memory kit consists of dual 24 GB DIMMs, which make up a total of 48 GB in capacity. Following are the full specifications of the kit I'll be testing.

  • Series: Opal DW100 RGB
  • Module Type: 288 Pin Unbuffered DIMM On-Die ECC
  • Capacity: 24GBx2
  • Frequency: 6000 Mbps
  • Data Transfer Bandwidth: 48.000 MB/s (PC5 48000)
  • Dimensions: 136.19 x 50.24 x 8.40 mm
  • Latency: CL28-36-36-72-108
  • Tested Voltage: 1.40V
  • Heat Spreader: Three-Fin Aluminum heatspreader

The memory kit has a tested latency spec of CL28, and the timings are 28-36-36-72-108 for this specific kit. The voltage set at reference is 1.40V, and the memory kit comes in an unbuffered package with a non-ECC (On-Die Error Correction) design.

The memory kit is fully compliant with AMD EXPO and Intel's XMP standards. DDR5 memory compatibility differs from motherboard to motherboard, so make sure that your board is high-end and rated to support high-clock-speed memory sticks.

As for detailed specs, there is just one EXPO profile that the Opal DW100 kit comes with. The first offers the aforementioned timings. Another thing to note is that the modules are based on the SK Hynix M-Die.

Biwin Opal DW100 RGB DDR5-6000 Memory Kit Unboxing and Close-Up

The Biwin Opal DW100 DDR5 RGB series memory comes in a standard cardboard package. The box has pictures of the memory in black and white colors, and lists various RGB software that it is compatible with. A stick also points out the specifications of the kit you are getting.

The back of the package lists that the kit comes with an express warranty and also offers a glance at the various features.

Within the package are two DDR5 DIMMs.

Outside the package, the Biwin Opal DW100 DDR5 RGB memory kit is a bulky and high-end design. The front has a three-fin heatsink design.

The kit we got comes in black. The memory also comes in all-black and all-white flavors.

Biwin Opal DW100 memory modules feature a full-coverage diffuser that covers the sides, too. It offers 16.8 million RGB colors, 10+ Lighting controls, and 8 distinct lighting zones.

The heat spreader is made using a 2mm aluminum alloy structure. Within this is a thermal pad that connects to each IC and the PMIC. Biwin also employs a Temperature Sensor, while the outside features an electrophoresis coating.

The frame of the memory DIMMs is of a high-class design that uses a matte finish.

Biwin also states that it is using an unlocked PMIC, which dynamically adjusts voltages on the DW100 series to maintain a stable power supply during overclocking. Furthermore, each DIMM features a 10-layer PCB that minimizes electromagnetic interference.

The following is how the memory looks when installed on an X670E motherboard.

Wccftech Memory Test Setup

For testing, I used the latest AMD Ryzen 7 9850X3D on the Gigabyte X670E AORUS Extreme motherboard using the latest BIOS with the AGESA 1.3.0.1b Path A firmware, which supports EXPO ULL memory kits. We also used an RTX 5090 for our tests.

DDR5 Memory Kit Performance Tests

I have separated the performance tests into two sections. The first section consists of synthetic and general workload tests, while the second section focuses on gaming performance and how different games are affected by higher-clocked memory sticks.

Gaming Performance Tests

Gaming applications generally don't see huge gains with different memory kits, but DDR5 has enabled a huge gain in memory frequency, which can impact gaming performance. Moving from the stock 2133 Mbps (DDR4) to 6000+ Mbps can have a huge effect on performance, and the improved CAS timings on some of the high-end SKUs can result in better overall FPS and system responsiveness. This is especially true for AMD's Ryzen CPUs and 3D V-Cache offerings that do benefit a lot from tighter-timed DDR5 kits.

We just hope that DDR5 prices return to normal as soon as possible so more PC users have access to these exceptional memory products.

item.title: G.Skill Trident Z5 NEOX RGB DDR5 32 GB (2 x 16 GB) 6000 MT/s CL36 EXPO ULL Memory Kit Review – Ultra Low Latency At A Time of Ultra High Pricing
item.description:
Product Info
G.Skill Trident Z5 NEOX RGB (16GB x 2 6000 CL36)
July, 2026
Type
Memory Kit
Price
$529.99

In 2026, we live in the worst kind of memory crisis that the consumer market has ever faced. Some people call it the RAMmageddon, and others call it the RAMpocalypse. Whichever way you want to call it, one thing is set: shortages created by massive demand in the AI and data center segment have led to a severe hike in prices across all memory segments.

This came at a time when memory speeds were seeing rapid updates. We saw speeds surge past 9000 MT/s, and some kits were hitting close to 10,000 MT/s. We saw the launch of CUDIMMs and CQDIMMs, plus capacities that pushed 2-DIMM platforms to 256 GB. But these are unobtanium these days. Even entry-level kits are out of reach.

AMD decided to do something different, knowing that the memory crisis won't go away anytime soon. They introduced a new standard for their EXPO technology called ULL, or Ultra-Low Latency. With its partners, the EXPO ULL tech delivers DDR5 kits with tighter timings that help CPUs gain additional performance. These kits were said to be available at around the same price points as regular EXPO kits. So today, we will be testing one of our first EXPO ULL kits, the G.Skill Trident Z5 NEOX.

G.Skill Trident Z5 NEOX RGB DDR5-6000 32 GB Memory Kit Specifications

The G.Skill Trident Z5 NEOX RGB memory kit retains the premium design and build quality that is known from the brand. We have tested many G.Skill memory kits over the years, and the Trident Z series remains one of the nicest-looking modules on the market.

For this particular testing, G.Skill sent us its Trident Z5 NEOX " F5-6000A3636F16GX2-TZ5NXRK " 32 GB (16 GB x 2) kit, which is rated at 6000 MT/s. The memory kit consists of dual 16 GB DIMMs, which make up a total of 32 GB in capacity. Following are the full specifications of the kit I'll be testing.

  • Series: CRAS V RGB
  • Module Type: 288 Pin Unbuffered DIMM On-Die ECC
  • Capacity: 16GBx2
  • Frequency: 6000 Mbps
  • Data Transfer Bandwidth: 48.000 MB/s (PC5 48000)
  • Dimensions: 44mm height
  • Latency: CL36-36-36-76
  • Tested Voltage: 1.35V
  • Heat Spreader: High-Quality Aluminum Heatsink

The memory kit has a tested latency spec of CL36, and the timings are 36-36-36-76 for this specific kit. The voltage set at reference is 1.35V, and the memory kit comes in an unbuffered package with a non-ECC (On-Die Error Correction) design.

The memory kit is fully compliant with AMD EXPO (ULL) and Intel's XMP standards. To enable EXPO ULL profiles, you need to use the latest AGESA 1.3.0.1b Path A or newer firmware. Memory compatibility differs from motherboard to motherboard, so make sure that your board is high-end and rated to support high-clock-speed memory sticks.

As for detailed specs, there are two EXPO profiles that the NEOX kit comes with. The first offers CL36-36-36-36-96-132 timings, while the second offers CL36-36-36-36-76-112 timings. The two profiles loaded instantly on our test X670E platform, which was running the latest BIOS. Another thing to note is that the modules are based on Samsung's B-Die.

G.Skill Trident Z5 NEOX RGB 32 GB DDR5-6000 Memory Kit Unboxing and Close-Up

The G.Skill Trident Z5 NEOX RGB series memory comes in a standard cardboard package. The box has the G.Skill Trident Z5 NEOX RGB and DDR5 labels on the front and also tells you that it is AMD EXPO ULL-compliant.

The back of the package lists that the kit comes with a limited lifetime warranty and gives you the latest instructions on how to enable the EXPO profile within the BIOS.

Within the package are two DDR5 DIMMs along with a G.Skill case sticker badge and a card that features a QR code that leads to the product page.

Outside the package, the G.Skill Trident Z5 NEOX RGB series offers a very aggressive & premium look with a high-end heat spreader. The design choice that G.Skill has gone with is very eye-catching and will look great even without the RGB LEDs.

G.Skill provided us with their matte-colored Trident Z5 NEOX RGB memory kit. The memory also comes in all-black and all-white flavors.

G.Skill experimented a lot with its diffuser system on the previous generation DDR4 Tridents. The new Tridents come in both RGB and Non-RGB flavors, but even without the LEDs, their design looks very premium, and the heat spreader and the multi-sided curve at the top really stand out from the rest of the DDR5 offerings.

The heat spreader makes use of a black aluminum strip inset into a metallic silver or matte black body and topped with a sleek black top bar.

The frame of the memory DIMMs is of a high-class design that uses polished aluminum heat spreaders of either black, white, or matte.

The memory modules are bulky and pack a solid heatsink that delivers optimal thermal performance. The sides feature a large Trident Z5 NEOX RGB logo. The DRAMs are fully covered by this aluminum-shielded heatsink, so higher temperatures won't be an issue on these sticks.

The following is how the memory looks when installed on an X670E motherboard.

Wccftech Memory Test Setup

For testing, I used the latest AMD Ryzen 7 9850X3D on the Gigabyte X670E AORUS Extreme motherboard using the latest BIOS with the AGESA 1.3.0.1b Path A firmware, which supports EXPO ULL memory kits. We also used an RTX 5090 for our tests.

DDR5 Memory Kit Performance Tests

I have separated the performance tests into two sections. The first section consists of synthetic and general workload tests, while the second section focuses on gaming performance and how different games are affected by higher-clocked memory sticks.

Gaming Performance Tests

Gaming applications generally don't see huge gains with different memory kits, but DDR5 has enabled a huge gain in memory frequency, which can impact gaming performance. Moving from the stock 2133 Mbps (DDR4) to 6000+ Mbps can have a huge effect on performance, and the improved CAS timings on some of the high-end SKUs can result in better overall FPS and system responsiveness. This is especially true for AMD's Ryzen CPUs and 3D V-Cache offerings that do benefit a lot from tighter-timed DDR5 kits.

Conclusion - A Solid Standard That Is Buried Beneath Ridiculous Prices

Through our tests, we found EXPO ULL kits to offer a 4-5% improvement over the standard EXPO kits in games, and a much bigger improvement over JEDEC-shipped kits such as 4800 and 5600 MT/s specs. While this is an improvement, and G.Skill's NEOX RGB with Samsung B-Dies are great, they unfortunately come at a time when memory prices are getting out of control.

The kit we tested retails for $529 , while the standard EXPO kit from G.Skill (NEO RGB) retails for $499. So not much of a difference there, but the price varies a lot as you go towards higher tiers, and that is true for all brands, not just G.Skill ( You can read G.Skill's comment on EXPO ULL price points here ). Furthermore, we should point out that even normal EXPO kits from G.Skill can be fine-tuned to reach these speeds. Sure, you are getting an out-of-the-box experience which is hassle-free, but experts can get tighter timings out of existing modules with ease.

Overall, I think the EXPO ULL DDR5 memory arrived way late. The standard should have been released last year when Ryzen 9000X3D CPUs were rolling out, and memory prices were still in control. The performance differences, although varying on a game-to-game basis, do show a lot of potential, and we will hope that prices return to normal so that more gamers and PC builders can get the most out of their PCs.

You can find additional information about our hardware review process and ethics policy here.

item.title: ASRock X870E Taichi White Motherboard Review – The Most Beautiful & Fully Decked-Out Taichi For Enthusiasts Under $500
item.description:
Product Info
ASRock X870E Taichi White
June, 2026
Type
Motherboard
Price
$439 US

It's been three years since the AMD AM5 motherboard platform and the 600-series motherboards launched. Since its launch, the platform has seen the launch of several CPUs in the Ryzen 7000, Ryzen 8000, and Ryzen 9000 families. While the 600-series motherboards provide great features & compatibility with newer Zen 5 CPUs, motherboard makers are always looking to enhance user experiences through the latest technologies, so AMD has introduced a new chipset line called the 800-series.

AMD also introduced both X870 and B850 series chipsets for high-end and mainstream AM5 motherboards. Both of these chips provide brand-new designs and brand-new features that are sure to entice new AMD builders and older AM4 users into investing in a new motherboard for their gaming and content creation needs.

We received a load of motherboards from various manufacturers, so let's start by taking a look at these updated designs. For this review, we will be testing out ASRock's latest X870E Taichi White , the most decked-out Taichi to date for AM5 builders with a retail price of $439.99.

The AMD AM5 Platform

The AMD AM4 platform has been the longest-running modern-day platform to date, which was first introduced in 2017 &  is very much alive and kicking in 2026. The company recently launched new chips for the platform, and it looks like it will stick around for a few more years.

But AMD has also introduced its 2nd Generation AM5 platform under the new 800-series family. The lineup initially targeted the high-end enthusiasts with the X870E and X870 chipsets. These chipsets are designed to offer better features, IO memory support, and additional OC features for Ryzen CPUs such as the Zen 5-based Ryzen 9000 "Granite Ridge" family and future Ryzen launches.

Certain aspects that have been upgraded on the AMD X870E & X870 motherboards include:

  • USB 4.0 standard on all X870/X870E motherboards
  • PCIe Gen5 on Graphics & NVME on all X870/X870E motherboards
  • Higher EXPO memory clock support on X870/X870E motherboards

AMD announced new PBO and CO algorithms introduced with Ryzen 9000 CPUs, and these new motherboards fully support them out of the box. In addition to the X870E & X870 motherboards, the company also introduced its B850 & B840 chipsets, which serve the mainstream segment. The following is what the chipset lineup will look like:

  • X870E (Promontory 21 x2)
  • X870 (Promontory 21 x1)
  • B650 (Promonotory 21 x1)
  • B840 (Promontory 19 x1)

So in terms of what each chipset offers, the X870E series uses two Promontory 21 dies on the motherboards with support for USB4 & both Gen5 GPU/SSD support. X870 (Non-E) features just 1 of the dies but retains the same support in terms of I/O. Just the lanes will be fewer.

AMD Chipset Features and Specifications:

Wccftech X870E X870 X670E/X670 B650E/B650 A620
CrossfireX/SLI 2-Way CFX 2-Way CFX 2-Way CFX 2-Way CFX N/A
CPU Lanes (Usable) 24 Gen 5 (with Ryzen 7000 CPUs & above) 24 Gen 5 (with Ryzen 7000 CPUs & above) 24 Gen 5 (with Ryzen 7000 CPUs & above) 24 Gen 5 (with Ryzen 7000 CPUs & above)
24 Gen 4 for B650 		24 Gen 4 (with Ryzen 7000 CPUs & above)
PCH Lanes (Usable) 8 Gen4
12 Gen3 		4 Gen4
8 Gen3 		12 Gen4
8 Gen3 		8 Gen4
4 Gen3 		8 Gen 3
USB4 Standard Standard Optional Optional Optional
USB 3.1/3.2 Gen2 2 1 2 1 0
USB 3.1/3.2 Gen1 12 6 12 6 2
USB 2.0 8 8 8 6 6
SATA 6Gb/s 8 8 8 4 4
DDR5 DIMMs 4 4 4 4 4
DDR4 DIMMs N/A N/A N/A N/A N/A
Overclocking
Support 		Yes Yes Yes Yes N/A
XFR2 Enhanced Yes Yes Yes Yes N/A
Precision Boost Overdrive Yes Yes Yes Yes N/A
NVMe Yes (Gen 5.0) Yes (Gen 5.0) Yes (Gen 5.0) Yes (Gen 5.0) N/A
Form Factor ATX/ITX ATX/mATX/ITX ATX/mATX/ITX ATX/mATX/ITX mATX/ITX

The motherboards feature support for DDR5-5600 MT/s memory speeds natively and over 8000 MT/s transfer rates on higher-end motherboards.

The AMD B850 motherboards feature the same Promontory 21 die, minus the native USB4 support. They carry Gen5 GPU support, but Gen5 M.2 support will be optional. The B840 will be the entry-level option within the series, with the older Promontory 19 die, which means you will get just Gen4 GPU/SSD support, no CPU overclocking, and only memory overclock support. The B840 motherboards are very affordable in terms of pricing, so they will be competitive against sub-$200 US options from Intel.

AMD also has the A620/A620A series, continuing its role to serve the entry-level segment. There are plans to offer even more price adjustments, making them a viable option for ODMs and system builders to attract more customers.

AMD AM5 Chipset Comparisons:

Chipset Name PCIe Lanes Gen (PCH) USB Support (Max) Overclocking Support Graphics Configs
X870E Gen5 (GPU & NVMe) USB4 CPU+Memory 1x16, 2x8
X670E Gen5 (GPU & NVMe) USB 3.2 (20 Gbps)
USB4 (Optional) 		CPU+Memory 1x16, 2x8
X870 Gen5 (GPU & NVMe) USB4 CPU+Memory 1x16, 2x8
X670 Gen5 (NVMe)
Gen4 (GPU) 		USB 3.2 (20 Gbps)
USB4 (Optional) 		CPU+Memory 1x16, 2x8
B850 Gen5 (NVMe / GPU Opt)
Gen4 (GPU) 		USB 3.2 (20 Gbps) CPU+Memory 1x16, 2x8
B650E Gen5 (NVMe / GPU) USB 3.2 (20 Gbps)
USB4 (Optional) 		CPU+Memory 1x16, 2x8
B650 Gen5 (NVMe)
Gen4 (GPU) 		USB 3.2 (20 Gbps)
USB4 (Optional) 		CPU+Memory 1x16, 2x8
B840 Gen3 (NVMe / GPU) USB 3.2 (10 Gbps) Memory Only 1x16
A620 Gen4 (NVMe / GPU) USB3.2 (10 Gbps)
USB4 (Optional) 		Memory Only 1x16

Meet The LGA 1718 Socket - How Long Will This One Last?

As mentioned earlier, AM4's reign is finally over, and the AM5 socket is here now. The new socket moves from a PGA (Pin-Grid-Array) design to an LGA (Land-Grid-Array) layout. The new LGA 1718 socket offers more pin connections to the CPU, allowing for more communication channels with the board itself and enabling support for enhanced features that the new platform has to offer.

As for longevity, AMD has committed to a 2029+ plan for its latest AM5 socket . The AM4 platform is a testament to AMD's support for its consumers, and while the newer 500-series lineup was locked out initially, the company and its partners worked towards extending support for older processors on the newer chipsets and newer CPUs on older motherboards. The AM4 platform continues to be the best seller in the Ryzen lineup, but that would change with the introduction of more budget- and feature-rich designs for the AM5 socket, like the 800-series.

Cooler Compatibility With AM5 Socket

The AMD Ryzen 7000/8000/9000 Desktop CPUs will feature a perfect square shape (45x45mm), but will house a very chunky integrated heat spreader or IHS. The CPUs will be the same length, width, and height as the existing Ryzen Desktop CPUs and are sealed across the sides, so applying thermal paste won't fill the interior of the IHS with TIM. That's also why current coolers will be fully compatible with the latest Ryzen CPUs.

Contents

  • 1. Intro
  • 2. ASRock X870E Taichi White - Unboxing & Close Up Inspection
  • 3. Test Setup
  • 4. CPU Performance Tests
  • 5. Gaming Performance Tests
  • 6. CPU Power Consumption Tests
  • 7. CPU & VRM Temperatures
  • 8. Conclusion - The Ultimate Taichi For Enthusiasts
Next page

item.title: Battlestar Galactica: Scattered Hopes Review – Sometimes, You Have to Roll a Hard six
item.description:
Game Info
Battlestar Galactica: Scattered Hopes
11th May, 2026
Platform
PC
Publisher
Dotemu
Developer
Alt Shift

Anybody who knows me knows I am a sucker for Sci-Fi, and two of my favourite space-based series are Star Trek and Battlestar Galactica. While this is a late review of the game, it's one that was sitting in my inbox and one that I felt that I should do for a few reasons: I'm a fan of the series, I enjoyed Star Trek Voyager: Across the Unknown when I reviewed it four months ago, and I am a fan of FTL-like games. Not picking up Battlestar Galactica: Scattered Hopes and covering it, whatever it turned out like, would clearly point to me being a toaster, and I would have to be treated as such.

From the above paragraph, you can probably guess what type of game Battlestar Galactica: Scattered Hopes is. It's a roguelite set in the BSG universe, similar to FTL. Set after the Cylon invasion of Caprica, you control a Gunstar and its crew, alongside a few civilian ships, as you flee the constant threat of the Cylons and make your way to the Galactica itself. Like the series and FTL, you are constantly being pursued, and time is not on your side, whether you're in the fleet management or the combat phase of the game.

Before I talk about the two gameplay areas, let's talk aesthetics. Battlestar Galactica: Scattered Hopes isn't trying to be a fully-realised 3D version of the show. This has a pixel art style that really works, and looks good for what it is. The limited rooms you can visit on your ship are well detailed and set the scene for whatever story has taken you there - or maybe you're just popping in to set some skills or upgrades - and the same applies to the space battles and their real-time-with-pause affair.

The same can be said for the subdued audio. There is no voice acting in this game, so a possible issue for some that I raised in the Star Trek Voyager review could also be one here, so be ready for another text-heavy game. Where it stands out is the music. While the game doesn't use any of the show's music, nor does it outright copy Bear McCreary's style, it really feels like it could be part of the BSG universe, just with a more techno vibe. It suits the game and universe exceptionally well and is worth a listen on its own.

I've already mentioned that there are two separate elements to gameplay: fleet management and combat. Fleet management is where you spend most of your time, spending ten turns in a system visiting points of interest, dealing with incidents, and levelling up your crew and ships before the Cylons catch up and you need to jump. The resource gathering and management isn't massively different to other similar titles, where you'll send a character (or use other resources) to head to a point of interest and get what's there. More often than not, it's different resources, but there are times when you'll be able to get new crew, weapons or fighters, or other times you may be able to pick up another vessel for your mini-fleet.

Other aspects you'll be balancing in the fleet management part of the game are also the morale of your fleet, as well as how happy the three factions are. If you lower a faction's favourability to you, you'll end up with a crisis (negative event); if you can rank a faction up and keep them happy, you'll get a positive event, and they will help you in some way during the final battle. Characters can also come with positive events if you are able to boost their morale enough, so as with the type of game - and as expected from the universe it's set in - everything is a balancing act.

Each new system you enter will also have a random crisis unless you are able to get one of the perks that can stop a crisis (can be gained through levelling up a specific type of character - characters also come in types and rarity). I can't say the events are exactly exciting or even that interesting, and even the more interesting one, where one of your crew is a Cylon, is just a case of filtering through the 'clues' to find them out. Your decision whether to attempt to redeem them or just kill them is arguably the most interesting aspect of that event.

It all makes for something that is more than enjoyable, even if it doesn't really stand out. Events are clearly borrowing, to an extent, from issues the fleet encounters in the show, but of course, have to be distinct enough not to require Starbuck or Tigh, as this is not their fleet. The atmosphere and pressure, despite it being text-based and essentially just a lot of reading, are what make the fleet management work as well as it does.

Adding to the pressure of resource, character and faction management is the combat. This is a more tactical affair, and not the fast-paced affair you will see in the show , and it's all the better for it. Given your art style and number limitations, and the sheer numbers the Cylons have, and the fact that missiles and nukes are being launched at you, it would become almost unmanageable. Instead it's not exactly chess, but you are still mostly looking at positioning and selecting the up to four fighters (if you upgrade your slots through R&D in fleet management) you take into combat.

You're essentially choosing between close-range fighters (like Vipers), ranged defenders, or support units. Vipers will chase an enemy in their range and duke it out. The ranged defenders cannot move and fire, so you want to position them well. Support units were functionally useless to me and were never used. Maybe I'm missing a trick, but I preferred to get to using four units, with two long-ranged defenders and two short-ranged fighters.

As you level your fighters up, they can also pick up both active and passive abilities, so you may find yourself using these alongside the weapons on your gunstar, so there is an active element to the combat. All of this is in a real-time-with-pause system, and the pause is absolutely essential as the Cylons aren't shy with their numbers, and the main enemy will be launching missiles and nukes at you.

Pausing will be even more important towards the end of a fight because the Cylons come on thick and fast, and trying to swarm you, and you're going to be watching the countdown until you can jump. Remember to make sure you get your planes back in the gunstar to save them from any damage, and as you can expect, you will want to limit the amount of damage your fleet and ships take. Too much damage, and you'll hit a crisis, and leave a pilot behind - or if they get destroyed in battle, they may have their own personal crisis or possibly even die.

Each run will take around two hours, which are quite long for this style of game. It didn't bother me much, but I can see it being an issue for others who are used to the quicker nature of roguelite runs. This time does, however, give you a chance to level up and prepare yourself for the final battle and a possible reunification with the Battlestar Galactica, and an in-game story explanation of why you don't appear in the show. The end of a run also gives you the chance to start with more resources, an increased chance of top-quality items, and more, making later runs that bit easier.

Battlestar Galactica: Scattered Hopes is, all around, an enjoyable FTL-like game in the BSG universe, and it does genuinely feel like it could be part of that universe. It's tense and engaging despite some repetitiveness, looks decent and sounds great, and is an all-around strong roguelite that I would recommend to fans of the sub-genre and fans of the universe it's set in, though the latter is not essential.

Copy provided by the publisher.

You can find additional information about our standard review process and ethics policy here.

7.5
WCCFTECH RATING

Battlestar Galactica: Scattered Hopes

Battlestar Galactica: Scattered Hopes is a strong entry into the FTL-like collection of games. While text-heavy, the tension of the BSG universe and fleeing the Cylons does come through, only amplified by excellent audio and a surprisingly fitting dark pixel-style art. There is some repetitiveness in the side-stories, but the strong gameplay and between-game upgrades can keep you coming back for more.

    Pros
  • Strong visual style with excellent audio design.
  • Keeps up the tension and atmosphere, and very fitting to the source material.
  • Strong gameplay elements throughout, with good resource management and progression.
  • Good replay value due to the nature of roguelite games...
    Cons
  • However, some side-stories can become repetitive quite quickly as they re-appear a lot.
  • Runs are on the long side for a roguelite style game.
Buy for from Amazon The links above are affiliate links. As an Amazon Associate, Wccftech.com may earn from qualifying purchases.

item.title: Noctua NL-LC1 420mm & 360mm AIO Coolers Review – The First AIOs From The Cooling Masters!
item.description:
Product Info
Noctua NL-LC1 360/420
June, 2026
Type
AIO Cooler
Price
$249/$279

The Austria-based cooler manufacturing company, Noctua, is a name renowned by several enthusiasts and overclockers in the PC industry. They are behind some of the most iconic PC coolers, such as the NH-D15 , the NH-U12 , and many more.

The company has been in action since 2005. and has become one of the most respected cooling product manufacturers in the industry with its legacy products. Since it entered the cooling segment, many competitors have come and vanished, but Noctua has endured, evolving its line to meet the latest requirements of the PC and DIY space.

Cooling equipment is still as relevant as ever, and many brands are now focusing on AIO (Closed-Loop) liquid coolers. It is kind of ironic that in 25+ years since its inception, Noctua hadn't rolled out a liquid cooler of its own. Considering their decades of engineering expertise in the cooling space, a Noctua-branded AIO cooler was long foreseen, but it took the company a lot of time to make one.

Last year at Computex, we finally got to see its prototype AIO cooler and also a new way to cool PCs, called Themosiphon . But even then, Noctua took its time to deliver a polished product that is now finally available on retail shelves. Meet the Noctua NL-LC1 , its first AIO liquid cooler, now rolling out in 240, 360, and 420mm flavors. For this review, Noctua was kind enough to send us their 360mm and 420mm variants of the NL-LC1, which retail at $249 and $279, respectively.

Noctua NL-LC1 AIO Liquid Cooler - Technical Details

So starting with the specifications first, the Noctua NL-LC1 lineup comes in 240mm, 360mm, and 420mm variants. comes with a 360mm radiator and is compatible with AMD AM4, AM5, and Intel LGA 1700, LGA 1851, and even the upcoming LGA 1954 sockets.

Noctua has gone with a black color scheme for the pump and radiator, while the fans come in the iconic Noctua brown colors. The company is also going to launch Chormax.Black variants of the AIO by the end of this year.

The package itself is very nicely arranged, with each component in its own compartment and labeled. The AIO also ships with a handy guide that lists the installation procedure for both AMD & Intel CPUs, and is very straightforward.

The Radiator on both coolers is made of aluminum fins and tubes, while the frame itself is a metal assembly. There are 20 Fins per inch, and the frame features UNC 6-32 screw threads.

The 420mm radiator measures 459.5 x 143.7 x 30mm and uses a triple-fan config (140x140x25), while the 360mm radiator measures 399.5 x 120 x 30mm and uses a triple-fan config that has a measurement of (120x120x25).

Both radiators are filled with 22% propylene glycol and 78% water. Noctua states that its coolant offers anti-freeze, anti-corrosion, and anti-biological protection while being non-toxic.

Noctua also ships its AIO coolers with an NL-ACF1 auxiliary cooling fan as a standalone accessory that replaces the magnetic pump faceplate. It is aimed at providing cooling for components such as the VRMs, RAM modules, and M.2 SSDs on the motherboard. The fan has a magnetic snap-on design and features an NF-A8 80mm fan with premium-grade SS02 fan bearings that guarantee over 150,000 hours of lifespan.

The fans come in their own cardboard box. The 420mm AIO features three NF-A14x25 G2 PWM fans which measure 140x140x25mm, offering speeds between 0-1500 RPM, and airflow of 91.58 CFM, 24.8 dbA of Audible noise (max), and 2.56mm H2O static pressure.

The 360mm AIO features three NF-A14x25 G2 PWM fans which measure 120x120x25mm, offering speeds between 0-1800 RPM, and airflow of 63.15 CFM, 22.5 dbA of Audible noise (max), and 3.14mm H2O static pressure.

Key specifications

All fans are rated at 12V and feature a single 4-pin connector to boot. Once again, these are brown-colored fans and Chromax.Black variants will be available later this year. All fans are backed by a 6-year warranty.

For the pump on the NL-LC1 series, Noctua is using the Asetek Emma V2, which features a 3-phase motor with a centrifugal impeller, ceramic sleeve, and shaft bearings, all enclosed in a case that measures 70mm tall. The pump has a max speed of 3400 RPM that can be adjusted down to 2600 RPM in balanced mode and 2100 RPM in quiet mode.

The pump produces 14.9 dBA noise in the manual mode, 7.8 dBA in balanced mode, and less than 5dBA in quiet mode. The pump uses a 4-pin PWM connector to operate and features a rated voltage of 12V with a max of 13.2V. Power is rated at 6W at max and 2W of typical input.

The pump is a bit taller than some standard AIO coolers, especially since it houses a VRM fan on the top (optional). Furthermore, the new Asetek design is highly efficient in terms of power.

The cold plate is made out of 99.95% Copper (CU1100) and has a size of 55.5 x 55.5mm which is ideal for all mainstream desktop CPUs. The construction materials include Skived fin micro-channels. For mounting, Noctua leverages its SecuFirm 2+ with a mounting hole pitch of 78mm. There's also an internal NL-PNA1 noise absorber.

The noise-dampening technology comes in 3 layers of soundproofing. First, it is the acoustic foam that sits within the pump, which acts as a dense sound barrier, while a floating silicone mount also helps ensure that the noise doesn't pass through the central 3-layer dampening structure.

The tubes themselves are lengthy, with 450mm of length for the 420mm and 410mm for the 360mm coolers. The sleeving is of a very premium quality, and the minimum bend radius is rated at 26mm.

Installation was simple. On our reference AMD platform, it took us around 10 minutes to get the cooler installed on the CPU, along with fan installation on the radiator.

ASRock Steel Legend 360 LCD 360mm AIO Liquid Cooler - Cooler Performance

For testing the performance, we used the AORUS X670E Xtreme motherboard paired with an AMD Ryzen 9 7950X3D CPU, 32 GB of G.Skill Trident Z5 NEO RGB memory (DDR5-6800 EXPO), and a ROG THOR II 1000W PSU. The chassis used was a Lian Li O11 Dynamic XL, which allowed us to mount the radiator on the top since the front is a mirror panel.
